<!-- Topic 1: Structures -->
<!-- Slides 3–33 -->

# Structures
<!-- Slide 3 -->

## One Employee, Three Variables {.smaller}

+ What keeps an ID, name, and hourly rate together as one employee?

::: notes
Slides 3–33
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## One Value Should Represent One Employee

+ Separate variables describe pieces of an employee.
+ A record treats those related pieces as one value.
+ Copying or moving the record keeps the pieces together.

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## Generic Structure Syntax

```cpp
struct TypeName {
    DataType memberName;
    DataType memberName;
};
```

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Employee Structure Anatomy

```cpp
struct Employee {
    int employeeID;
    string name;
    double hourlyRate;
};
```

::: notes
Label the keyword, new type name, member declarations, braces, and required semicolon.
:::

<!-- Slide 7 -->

---

## A Definition Does Not Create an Employee

```cpp
struct Employee {
    int employeeID;
    string name;
    double hourlyRate;
};

Employee manager;
Employee assistant;
```

<!-- Slide 8 -->

---

## Defining and Declaring

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

struct Employee {
    int employeeID;
    string name;
    double hourlyRate;
};

int main() {
    Employee worker;
    cout << sizeof(worker) << '\n';
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 9 -->

---

## Accessing Members

```cpp
Employee worker {104, "Ana Ruiz", 27.50};

cout << worker.employeeID << '\n';
cout << worker.name << '\n';
cout << worker.hourlyRate << '\n';
```

<!-- Slide 10 -->

---

## Assigning Members

```cpp
Employee worker;

worker.employeeID = 104;
worker.name = "Ana Ruiz";
worker.hourlyRate = 27.50;
```

<!-- Slide 11 -->

---

## Reading a Structure from Input

```cpp
int main() {
    Employee worker;

    cout << "Employee ID: ";
    cin >> worker.employeeID;
    cin.ignore();
    cout << "Name: ";
    getline(cin, worker.name);
    cout << "Hourly rate: ";
    cin >> worker.hourlyRate;

    cout << worker.name << ": $"
         << worker.hourlyRate << '\n';
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 12 -->

---

## Aggregate Initialization

```cpp
Employee first {104, "Ana Ruiz", 27.50};
Employee second {205, "Ben Lee", 31.25};
```

+ Values correspond to members in declaration order.

<!-- Slide 13 -->

---

## Initialization Follows Declaration Order

```cpp
struct Measurement {
    double temperature;
    int sampleNumber;
};

Measurement reading {7, 21};
```

+ What values and types will the two members receive?

<!-- Slide 14 -->

---

## Partial and Value Initialization

```cpp
Employee unknown {};
Employee intern {301, "Kai"};
```

+ Omitted numeric members are value-initialized to zero.
+ `{}` value-initializes every member.

<!-- Slide 15 -->

---

## Copying a Structure

```cpp
Employee original {104, "Ana Ruiz", 27.50};
Employee copy = original;

copy.hourlyRate = 30.00;

cout << original.hourlyRate << '\n';
cout << copy.hourlyRate << '\n';
```

<!-- Slide 16 -->

---

## Comparing Structures

```cpp
bool sameEmployee(const Employee& left,
                  const Employee& right);

bool sameEmployee(const Employee& left,
                  const Employee& right) {
    return left.employeeID == right.employeeID;
}
```

+ Decide which members define equality for the program.

<!-- Slide 17 -->

---

## Passing a Structure by Value

```cpp
void giveRaise(Employee worker) {
    worker.hourlyRate += 2.00;
}

Employee worker {104, "Ana Ruiz", 27.50};
giveRaise(worker);
cout << worker.hourlyRate << '\n';
```

::: notes
Trace the independent parameter copy.
:::

<!-- Slide 18 -->

---

## Passing by `const` Reference

```cpp
void printEmployee(const Employee& worker) {
    cout << worker.employeeID << "  "
         << worker.name << "  $"
         << worker.hourlyRate << '\n';
}
```

+ Use a `const` reference when a function only reads a record.

<!-- Slide 19 -->

---

## Modifying through a Reference

```cpp
void giveRaise(Employee& worker, double amount) {
    worker.hourlyRate += amount;
}

Employee worker {104, "Ana Ruiz", 27.50};
giveRaise(worker, 2.00);
cout << worker.hourlyRate << '\n';
```

<!-- Slide 20 -->

---

## Returning a Structure

```cpp
Employee readEmployee();

int main() {
    Employee worker = readEmployee();
    return 0;
}

Employee readEmployee() {
    Employee worker;
    cin >> worker.employeeID;
    cin.ignore();
    getline(cin, worker.name);
    cin >> worker.hourlyRate;
    return worker;
}
```

<!-- Slide 21 -->

---

## Structures Can Contain Structures

```cpp
struct Date {
    int month;
    int day;
    int year;
};

struct Employee {
    int employeeID;
    string name;
    Date hireDate;
};

cout << worker.hireDate.year << '\n';
```

<!-- Slide 22 -->

---

## A Complete Employee Program

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

struct Employee {
    int employeeID;
    string name;
    double hourlyRate;
};

Employee makeEmployee(int id, string name, double rate);
void giveRaise(Employee& worker, double amount);
void printEmployee(const Employee& worker);

int main() {
    Employee worker = makeEmployee(104, "Ana Ruiz", 27.50);
    giveRaise(worker, 2.00);
    printEmployee(worker);
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 23 -->

---

## A Complete Employee Program

```cpp
Employee makeEmployee(int id, string name, double rate) {
    Employee worker {id, name, rate};
    return worker;
}

void giveRaise(Employee& worker, double amount) {
    worker.hourlyRate += amount;
}

void printEmployee(const Employee& worker) {
    cout << worker.employeeID << "  "
         << worker.name << "  $"
         << worker.hourlyRate << '\n';
}
```

<!-- Slide 24 -->

---

## How Much Memory Does a Structure Use?

```cpp
struct Sample {
    char code;
    int reading;
};

cout << sizeof(char) + sizeof(int) << '\n';
cout << sizeof(Sample) << '\n';
```

+ Predict both outputs before running the program.

<!-- Slide 25 -->

---

## Padding and Alignment

+ Members may need addresses aligned for efficient access.
+ The compiler may insert unused padding bytes.
+ `sizeof(StructType)` includes padding.
+ Member order can change the structure’s size.

<!-- Slide 26 -->

---

## When Should Data Become a Structure?

+ The values describe one entity.
+ The values move through functions together.
+ Copying one value should copy the complete record.
+ An array cannot represent the fields because their types differ.

<!-- Slide 27 -->

---

## Where Should the Definition Go?

```cpp
struct Employee {
    int employeeID;
    string name;
    double hourlyRate;
};

void printEmployee(const Employee& worker);

int main() {
    Employee worker {};
    printEmployee(worker);
    return 0;
}
```

+ Define the type before prototypes that use it.

<!-- Slide 28 -->

---

## Default Values Protect New Records

```cpp
struct Employee {
    int employeeID = 0;
    string name = "Unknown";
    double hourlyRate = 0.0;
};

Employee worker;
cout << worker.name << '\n';
```

<!-- Slide 29 -->

---

## Structures Can Contain Arrays

```cpp
struct Student {
    string name;
    int scores[3] {};
};

Student student {"Mina", {88, 91, 95}};
cout << student.scores[1] << '\n';
```

<!-- Slide 30 -->

---

## Pointers to Structures

```cpp
Employee worker {104, "Ana Ruiz", 27.50};
Employee* workerPtr = &worker;

cout << (*workerPtr).name << '\n';
cout << workerPtr->name << '\n';

workerPtr->hourlyRate += 2.00;
```

<!-- Slide 31 -->

---

## A Structure Cannot Protect Itself Yet

```cpp
Employee worker;

worker.employeeID = -9;
worker.name = "";
worker.hourlyRate = -500.00;
```

+ Public members allow every part of the program to create invalid states.
+ This limitation motivates abstract data types and classes.

<!-- Slide 32 -->

---

## Structures Summary

+ A structure defines a new type by grouping related members.
+ Dot notation selects a member; whole-record assignment copies every member.
+ Choose value, `const` reference, or reference parameters according to the function’s purpose.

<!-- Slide 33 -->
